title: Kiss My Butler

jokes.text: And they say the man can't party.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey describes his birthday plans: watching BBC Gilbert & Sullivan opera followed by a David Frost interview
jokes.punchline: Will's sarcastic commentary on how boring these plans sound
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Will's streetwise perspective clashing with Geoffrey's refined tastes. The deadpan delivery and economy make it land well.

jokes.text: Carlton, put away that tired old kazoo, please.
jokes.setup: The family is preparing something special for Geoffrey's birthday
jokes.punchline: Will dismisses Carlton's attempt to contribute music with a kazoo as inadequate
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Will taking charge and Carlton's earnest but inadequate contributions. Fairly standard sibling dynamic joke.

jokes.text: Geoffrey's reaction to the rap - 'He just lit up'
jokes.setup: Will performs an enthusiastic birthday rap for Geoffrey
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's presumably reserved British reaction being described as 'lighting up'
jokes.explanation: Works on the contrast between Geoffrey's reserved nature and what counts as excitement for him.

jokes.text: How'd you like to go into the kitchen and introduce Mr. Tea Bag and Mr. Teapot to Mr. Boiling Water?
jokes.setup: Hilary needs to make tea for Geoffrey
jokes.punchline: Vivian talks to adult Hilary like a small child with cutesy names for kitchen items
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Vivian's frustration with Hilary's helplessness, expressed through condescending baby talk.

jokes.text: Hilary, how'd you like to make the tea before I take away Mr. Allowance?
jokes.setup: Vivian just used the 'Mr.' naming convention to talk down to Hilary
jokes.punchline: Philip escalates with a threat using the same condescending format
jokes.explanation: Nice quick escalation that builds on the previous joke while adding Philip's parental authority.

jokes.text: Mother, please. Don't talk down to me.
jokes.setup: Parents are literally talking down to Hilary like a child
jokes.punchline: Hilary's oblivious response missing that she IS being talked down to because she acts like a child
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary character moment - she's completely unaware of her own childishness while complaining about being treated like a child.

jokes.text: Little Richard?
jokes.setup: Will says he's getting Geoffrey something for his birthday and offers a hint
jokes.punchline: Carlton's completely wrong guess reveals his cultural disconnect
jokes.explanation: Economical character comedy - Carlton's sheltered background makes him completely miss Will's obvious hint about getting Geoffrey a woman.

jokes.text: No, he's not depressed. He's British.
jokes.setup: Will says Geoffrey seems depressed staying home alone
jokes.punchline: Philip's explanation that Geoffrey's demeanor is just his nationality
jokes.explanation: Excellent observational humor with perfect economy. The equation of British reserve with depression is both culturally observant and character-specific for Philip.

jokes.text: Your poor Aunt Vivian seems to think she's a matchmaker. In her mind, she's the Chuck Woolery of Bel-Air.
jokes.setup: Vivian suggests finding Geoffrey a nice woman
jokes.punchline: Philip's specific pop culture reference to the Love Connection host
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Philip's eye-rolling at his wife's romantic meddling, elevated by the specific Chuck Woolery reference.

jokes.text: When I was in college, I introduced three couples and they all got married. And by your fifth reunion, they were all divorced. I just get them to the altar. After that, they're on their own.
jokes.setup: Vivian brags about her matchmaking success in college
jokes.punchline: Philip reveals all her matches ended in divorce, Vivian's cavalier response
jokes.explanation: Well-structured joke with a dark twist. Vivian's casual dismissal of the divorces adds character depth and comedy.

jokes.text: People, people, please. I don't want to hear your dirty laundry.
jokes.setup: Philip and Vivian are arguing about her matchmaking track record
jokes.punchline: Will uses 'dirty laundry' literally in context of Geoffrey's chores while referencing the idiom
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay connecting the idiom to Geoffrey's actual laundry duties, though not particularly original.

jokes.text: The mall.
jokes.setup: Will announces he knows exactly where to find Geoffrey a serious woman
jokes.punchline: His answer is the most superficial possible location
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with perfect economy. Classic Will logic - completely missing the point while being confident.

jokes.text: I can see myself. I can't.
jokes.setup: Carlton has polished something so well he can see himself in it
jokes.punchline: Hilary's self-deprecating response about her own appearance
jokes.explanation: Quick character moment showing Hilary's vanity through self-deprecation, delivered with good economy.

jokes.text: Hilary, you can't ask him to wrap his own present. I guess that's another one of those unwritten rules.
jokes.setup: Hilary considers asking Geoffrey to wrap his own gift
jokes.punchline: Hilary treating basic social etiquette as mysterious unwritten rules
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Hilary's cluelessness about social norms she should know.

jokes.text: What did you expect? You went to the mall with girl-away there.
jokes.setup: Will complains he didn't meet any girls at the mall
jokes.punchline: Carlton suggests Hilary's presence repelled women with a play on 'getaway'
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay from Carlton showing his rivalry with Hilary while explaining Will's failure.

jokes.text: Sure, maybe during a blackout.
jokes.setup: Jazz claims to be irresistible to women
jokes.punchline: Hilary suggests he'd only be attractive when women can't see him
jokes.explanation: Standard insult comedy with good delivery and economy, though not particularly original.

jokes.text: I'd love to get a hold of you during a blackout.
jokes.setup: Hilary just mentioned a blackout scenario
jokes.punchline: Jazz turns her insult into a sexual advance
jokes.explanation: Predictable escalation that relies on innuendo rather than wit. Character-appropriate for Jazz but not clever.

jokes.text: Daddy.
jokes.setup: Jazz makes a sexual advance on Hilary
jokes.punchline: Hilary immediately calls for her father like a child
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character work - adult Hilary's childlike response to being hit on.

jokes.text: In 55 seconds. Just tell us when to zap it.
jokes.setup: Asked when their elaborate dinner will be ready
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's all microwave food despite fancy description
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of expectations - the fancy menu description undercut by microwave reality, very Hilary.

jokes.text: The words 'mission impossible' come to mind.
jokes.setup: Will suggests finding Geoffrey a girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Philip's reference to the difficulty being insurmountable
jokes.explanation: Standard pop culture reference joke, functional but not particularly creative.

jokes.text: A lonely woman, new to our country... she probably won't even notice what a Melvin Geoffrey is.
jokes.setup: Learning the nanny has only been in America two weeks
jokes.punchline: Will's calculation that her inexperience makes Geoffrey viable
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Will's street-smart logic applied to Geoffrey's romantic prospects. 'Melvin' is perfect slang choice.

jokes.text: So far, I count you plus three guys.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey describes his evening plans with Gilbert, Sullivan, and David Frost
jokes.punchline: Will points out these are all male names, implying Geoffrey's social life lacks women
jokes.explanation: Clever observation that reframes Geoffrey's cultured evening plans as a sausage fest.

jokes.text: I'm not making too big an assumption here, am I?
jokes.setup: Will suggests Geoffrey might prefer a woman's company
jokes.punchline: Will's awkward follow-up questioning Geoffrey's sexuality
jokes.explanation: Good awkward moment showing Will's directness creating uncomfortable territory.

jokes.text: Surely you gleaned that much from watching Mr. Belvedere.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey explains why butlers shouldn't mix personal and professional life
jokes.punchline: Reference to another show about a British butler with a family
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-humor having Geoffrey reference another sitcom butler, showing self-awareness of the character type.

jokes.text: I mean, it's your patriotic duty to show your countrywoman a good time.
jokes.setup: Will is trying to convince Geoffrey to go on the date
jokes.punchline: Reframes reluctant dating as British patriotic duty
jokes.explanation: Creative persuasion tactic from Will, appealing to Geoffrey's British identity in a humorous way.

jokes.text: I wouldn't want to disappoint King Elizabeth.
jokes.setup: Will appeals to Geoffrey's British patriotic duty
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's formal acceptance referencing the wrong monarch's gender
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment but the King/Queen error feels like a minor mistake rather than intentional comedy.

jokes.text: Don't be such a wanker.
jokes.setup: Will is trying to help Geoffrey get ready
jokes.punchline: American Will using British slang incorrectly/inappropriately
jokes.explanation: Good culture clash humor with Will trying to sound British and probably using the term inappropriately.

jokes.text: You got a kind of a Ben Vereen thing going here, man.
jokes.setup: Will is critiquing Geoffrey's outfit choice
jokes.punchline: Specific reference to the entertainer known for flashy performance wear
jokes.explanation: Period-appropriate pop culture reference that works for Will's character giving fashion advice.

jokes.text: Pure, unadulterated funk.
jokes.setup: Will is trying to make Geoffrey look 'funkier'
jokes.punchline: Will's proud declaration of the presumably ridiculous result
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in imagining what Geoffrey looks like after Will's makeover and Will's satisfaction with it.

jokes.text: During the '70s, I was briefly employed as Led Zeppelin's road butler.
jokes.setup: Will is trying to teach Geoffrey about being cool
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey reveals an unexpectedly rock-and-roll past job
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character subversion - the absurd concept of a 'road butler' for Led Zeppelin perfectly contrasts Geoffrey's stuffiness with unexpected coolness. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I would be most happy to, but to the best of my knowledge... hell has not yet frozen over.
jokes.setup: Will asks Geoffrey to try dancing
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's formal British way of saying 'hell no'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Geoffrey character work - taking a common expression and delivering it with formal British politeness. Excellent contrast.

jokes.text: I won't answer that without a lawyer and a dictionary.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey asks if Will must be so 'ribald'
jokes.punchline: Will doesn't know what the word means but assumes it's bad
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy showing Will's street smarts vs. formal vocabulary gap. The lawyer/dictionary combo is clever.

jokes.text: Okay, then I'll keep the sweater.
jokes.setup: Philip suggests Hilary could stop bossing Geoffrey around instead of giving gifts
jokes.punchline: Hilary immediately chooses to keep the gift rather than change her behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary character moment - completely missing the point and choosing the selfish option instantly.

jokes.text: Get ready to have your candles blown out.
jokes.setup: It's Geoffrey's birthday with Helen arriving as his date
jokes.punchline: Will's sexual innuendo using birthday candle metaphor
jokes.explanation: Standard double entendre that's character-appropriate for Will but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Ditto.
jokes.setup: Helen says Geoffrey seems younger than the children described, expecting him to be older
jokes.punchline: Helen's casual 'ditto' suggests she's also younger/more attractive than expected
jokes.explanation: Economical character moment that sets up the mutual attraction and subverts expectations.

jokes.text: What are you trying to pull here, Tony the Tiger?
jokes.setup: Carlton introduces himself to Helen as 'Tony'
jokes.punchline: Will's cereal mascot reference mocking Carlton's fake name
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture reference that works for calling out Carlton's obvious attempt to seem cooler to Helen.

jokes.text: She'll be dancing with him, but she'll be thinking of me.
jokes.setup: Carlton is clearly attracted to Helen despite her being Geoffrey's date
jokes.punchline: Carlton's delusional confidence about Helen's interest
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton character moment showing his overconfidence and romantic delusions.

jokes.text: Ashley, this must be that houseboy you were telling me about. That's homeboy. Sorry.
jokes.setup: Helen misunderstands American slang 'homeboy' as 'houseboy'
jokes.punchline: The correction and Helen's polite apology for the mistake
jokes.explanation: Good cultural misunderstanding humor that works for Helen's British background misinterpreting American slang.

jokes.text: It has been a while.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey and Helen realize they're from the same exact London neighborhood
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's understated reaction to this remarkable coincidence
jokes.explanation: Perfect British understatement for what's clearly meant to be a huge coincidence in the setup.

jokes.text: You both speak English.
jokes.setup: Vivian comments on how much Geoffrey and Helen have in common
jokes.punchline: Hilary's painfully obvious observation about their shared language
jokes.explanation: Classic Hilary obliviousness - stating the most obvious thing as if it's insightful.

jokes.text: You both speak English.
jokes.setup: Vivian gushes about Geoffrey and Helen having so much in common
jokes.punchline: Philip's dry observation that their only commonality is language
jokes.explanation: Perfect Philip deadpan deflating Vivian's matchmaking enthusiasm. Excellent economy and dry delivery.

jokes.text: You're not the first to have made that comparison.
jokes.setup: Helen compares Geoffrey to James Bond after hearing his stories
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's modest acceptance suggesting this comparison happens often
jokes.explanation: Good character work building on Geoffrey's mysterious past while showing his modest pride.

jokes.text: Geoffrey, this is fascinating. It's like going out with James Bond.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey has been telling stories about his adventures
jokes.punchline: Helen compares the butler to the famous spy
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Geoffrey's hidden exciting past. Solid pop culture reference.

jokes.text: Look. That's Tom Cruise's pool cleaner. And that's Cher's electrolysist. And that's Heather Locklear.
jokes.setup: Hilary claims the 'A-list' turned out to the club
jokes.punchline: Two celebrity service providers followed by an actual celebrity as anticlimax
jokes.explanation: Clever structure with escalating absurdity of celebrity connections, then deflating with actual celebrity treated casually.

jokes.text: What's with the Band-Aid? Was your brain leaking?
jokes.setup: Jazz has a bandage on his head from being thrown out of the house
jokes.punchline: Hilary's cutting insult suggesting the injury exposed his stupidity
jokes.explanation: Sharp insult comedy that works for Hilary's character - cutting but creative rather than just mean.

jokes.text: I love a flirt.
jokes.setup: Hilary has just delivered a harsh rejection and insult
jokes.punchline: Jazz completely misreads her hostility as flirting
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jazz character moment - his delusional interpretation of clear rejection fits his persistent character.

jokes.text: There's Demi Moore's cable man.
jokes.setup: Continues the pattern of celebrity service providers being noteworthy
jokes.punchline: Another random celebrity connection treated as important
jokes.explanation: Decent callback that maintains the pattern but lacks the escalation structure of the original.

jokes.text: All the babes I mess with in Bel-Air been whacked, right?
jokes.setup: Will is explaining his romantic luck in Bel-Air
jokes.punchline: His assessment that all attractive women he's encountered have been crazy
jokes.explanation: Good character observation about Will's dating pattern in this environment, sets up his dilemma.

jokes.text: I don't know what you guys do in Philly... but in LA, we don't give stuff like that away.
jokes.setup: Will says Geoffrey didn't even want to go out with Helen
jokes.punchline: Jazz's regional stereotype about LA competitiveness vs Philly generosity
jokes.explanation: Good regional rivalry setup that establishes the philosophical difference between the friends.

jokes.text: I don't know what you guys do in Philly, but in LA, we men have to take that risk.
jokes.setup: Will threatens violence over Jazz pursuing Helen
jokes.punchline: Jazz doubles down on the regional comparison while accepting the danger
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the regional rivalry while building tension for the scheme.

jokes.text: I don't know what you guys do in LA, but in Philly, you crack on somebody's honey, you get yourself killed.
jokes.setup: Jazz wants to move in on Geoffrey's date
jokes.punchline: Will's regional pride and threat about Philadelphia dating rules
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Will's loyalty to Geoffrey and Philadelphia street code. Sets up the regional rivalry.

jokes.text: Like an angel in flight. Yeah, he is better than I thought he'd be.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey and Helen are dancing, presumably very well
jokes.punchline: Will and Jazz's grudging admission that Geoffrey is actually a good dancer
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing their surprise at Geoffrey's skill, but not particularly strong comedy.

jokes.text: They're doing the lambada.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey and Helen are dancing well together
jokes.punchline: Will identifies their dance as the sensual 'forbidden dance'
jokes.explanation: Period pop culture reference that works for the surprise of Geoffrey's dancing ability.

jokes.text: That's the forbidden dance.
jokes.setup: Will mentions the lambada
jokes.punchline: Jazz provides the famous tagline from the movie
jokes.explanation: Straightforward pop culture reference callback, functional but not particularly creative.

jokes.text: Geoffrey does the lambada? That's the forbidden dance.
jokes.setup: Will explains why he got kicked out - Geoffrey's lambada dancing
jokes.punchline: Carlton's shocked reaction and Will repeating the tagline
jokes.explanation: Callback that reinforces Geoffrey's surprising dance skills but relies on repeated reference.

jokes.text: Back flips?
jokes.setup: Hilary mentions Geoffrey was doing back flips while dancing
jokes.punchline: Carlton's incredulous reaction to this physical impossibility for Geoffrey
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Geoffrey's surprising physical abilities with Carlton's shocked reaction.

jokes.text: I guess what I want to say is it's all Jazz's fault, man.
jokes.setup: Will is trying to apologize to Geoffrey for interfering at the club
jokes.punchline: Will deflects responsibility to Jazz instead of taking blame
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Will's immaturity in avoiding responsibility during his apology.

jokes.text: I shouldn't have busted your groove at the club.
jokes.setup: Will is apologizing to Geoffrey for interfering with his date
jokes.punchline: Will's use of slang 'busted your groove' in a sincere apology
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Will's genuine remorse expressed in his natural slang. Solid character work.

jokes.text: For example, if I were to judge you by your costume... I should guess you were some kind of circus performer.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey is explaining not to judge people by appearances
jokes.punchline: His pointed insult about Will's clothing while making a moral point
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work - Geoffrey delivers a moral lesson while simultaneously roasting Will's fashion sense with perfect British politeness.

jokes.text: That's pretty cold, G.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey just insulted Will's outfit while making a moral point
jokes.punchline: Will's acknowledgment that Geoffrey got him good
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat showing Will can appreciate being roasted when it's done well.

jokes.text: I am, as the saying goes... a fairly fly individual.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey is explaining he's proven himself cool
jokes.punchline: His formal British delivery of American slang 'fly'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy - Geoffrey adopting Will's slang but delivering it with perfect British formality. The contrast is hilarious and perfectly executed.

jokes.text: She's certainly a hot little number.
jokes.setup: Will mentions Helen thinks Geoffrey is great
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey using American slang to describe Helen
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of Geoffrey adopting American slang, though less surprising than the previous example.

jokes.text: I am afraid I was a little too intimidating for the poor girl.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey explains why he and Helen were mismatched
jokes.punchline: His humble-brag about being too impressive for her
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Geoffrey's pride disguised as concern for Helen's feelings.

jokes.text: I could be slower. I'm sure I could be less sophisticated.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey says Helen needs someone slower and less sophisticated
jokes.punchline: Will's eager offer to dumb himself down for her
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Will's eagerness to get the girl, even if it means admitting to being unsophisticated.

tmdb_overview: Happy birthday Geoffrey! For a gift, Will tries matchmaking, then falls for Geoffrey's date himself.
